question_text: A crane uses a long horizontal beam (jib) with a pivot point (fulcrum) near one end. The heavy load hangs far from the pivot, while a counterweight is placed on the opposite side close to the pivot. Explain why this design is effective and identify what would happen if the counterweight were moved even closer to the pivot without being made heavier.
correct_answer: The design is effective because even a relatively light counterweight can balance a much heavier load by placing it far from the pivot, using the principle of moments. If the counterweight were moved closer to the pivot without being made heavier, its moment would decrease (since moment = force × distance). This would mean the clockwise moment from the heavy load would be greater than the anticlockwise moment from the counterweight, causing the crane to become unbalanced and tip towards the load side.
explanation: This is a complex problem requiring understanding of how moment varies with distance, and the ability to predict what happens when the principle of moments is broken. Students must apply their knowledge to a new, realistic scenario and reason through the consequences of changing a variable.
